# Hrushikesh Deshmukh — Senior Software Engineer (MS CS)

> Applying to: OpenAI · Software Engineer, Enterprise AI Platform (EPT AI Pod)
> This variant frames my work for the EPT context. Canonical resume at /resume.pdf
> All audience variants listed at /variants

## Why this role

The JD describes work I'm doing today and the work I'm moving toward. I ship
a production MCP server to internal and third-party audiences through Amazon's
enterprise build infrastructure, and I designed a multi-agent orchestration
framework for developer tooling. Both are in production at Amazon Devices.

The interesting engineering isn't in the AI itself — it's in the enterprise
distribution path, the stakeholder alignment, the dual-audience versioning,
and the reusable framework I built to unblock GA when a prior owner left
scope errors behind. That's the same problem shape EPT solves for Finance,
People, and GTM workflows.

## Most relevant work

### 1. Production MCP server: @amazon-devices/amazon-devices-buildertools-mcp

- Public npm package, [K] months in production
- Dual-fork distribution: internal Amazon consumers + external third-party
  device makers, both from a single source of truth
- The MCP itself is intentionally simple (static prompts + bundled context).
  Reliability and audit-readiness over novelty.

**Security model: content firewall, not runtime auth**
- No runtime authentication because the public artifact is structurally
  incapable of containing internal content.
- Guardrails in the publish pipeline statically verify the public fork:
  no internal API references, no Amazon-private prompts, no internal-only
  tool descriptions can make it into the artifact that ships to npm.
- This is the right shape of security: prevent sensitive content from
  entering the artifact, rather than gating access to an artifact that
  already contains it.

**Development workflow: spec-driven with Claude**
- Designed spec-first. The spec is the source of truth from which both
  forks generate. This is what keeps the dual-fork model maintainable.
- I treat AI assistance as a design partner for spec'd work and a
  pair-programmer for code, not as a code generator.

**Why this work is harder than it looks**
- The package is public-facing. The stakeholder set includes Solutions
  Architects (who represent customer reality), support teams (who own
  post-launch), developer evangelists (who own positioning), and product
  leadership (who own prioritization).
- These stakeholders are strongly opinionated and their priorities compete.
  The work of aligning them is non-linear — multiple workshops, dog-fooding
  sessions with internal and external developers, iterative feedback cycles.
- The hardest part was not the MCP. It was navigating the enterprise
  stakeholder landscape to ship something public-facing through an
  organization that moves carefully.
- Same problem shape as the EPT pod: building reliable internal products
  while partnering with system owners and business owners who have
  competing definitions of "done."

### 2. Reusable infrastructure: the @amzn → @amazon-devices rescoping framework

- One month before GA, the device-platform release was about to ship blocked:
  prior owner (now at a PE firm) had created [N] packages under Amazon's
  internal-only @amzn scope. Internal scope cannot publish to public npm.
- I designed a release-pipeline framework that rewrites @amzn → @amazon-devices
  during publish, preserves dependency graphs, and validates the rescope
  before npm push. Not a one-off fix — reusable infrastructure.
- Unblocked GA on time. The framework is now the standard publish path for
  [X] other packages in the same pipeline.
- In the JD's words: "reusable infrastructure, patterns, and components
  that other enterprise teams can build on."

### 3. Multi-agent orchestration, in two parts

**Part A — Production: SDK team AI tools (developer tooling)**
- Internal package I designed and own end-to-end; used daily by ~[N] engineers
- I built: the skill contract (extension API), the router/orchestrator agent,
  and the sub-agent integration pattern
- Architecture: router-and-skills with specialized sub-agents
  - SDK agent — top-level router/dispatcher
  - Skills — modular capability extensions; the team adds them, not me
  - Sub-agents — domain specialists (build-tools, Amazon/Devices org agents)
- Honest scope: multi-agent architecture for human-in-the-loop developer
  workflows. Consumers are ~few hundred internal devs working in agentic
  harnesses (Claude Code, kiro-cli). Not fully autonomous multi-agentic.
- Approval flows: delegated to the user's harness. Those harnesses already
  implement approval UX; adding another layer would be redundant. If a
  specific skill needs approval beyond what the harness provides, the
  responsibility belongs to the skill, not the orchestrator. Composition
  over centralization.
- Observability: deliberate non-investment. Agents act on behalf of
  developers via downstream systems (build, auth, etc.) that are already
  observable. Adding agent-level traces would duplicate signal at real cost.

**Part B — Exploratory: AgentCore build automation**
- Currently building a fully autonomous multi-agentic system on Amazon
  Bedrock AgentCore for build automation: [specific scope]
- Autonomous agents acting on systems without a developer in the loop is
  a fundamentally different problem category. Here, agent-level
  observability isn't optional — the orchestration IS the system you
  need to observe.
- Why AgentCore: first-class observability, identity, and gateway primitives
  that are structural to autonomous multi-agent systems, not retrofitted.
- Part A proves I can architect multi-agent; Part B is me building in the
  same category EPT plays in.

### 4. Enterprise build-pipeline bridge: Brazil/Builder Hub → public npm

- Authored custom adapters that translate Amazon-internal build assumptions
  (versioning, scope, metadata, dependency resolution) into npm-compatible
  artifacts.
- Led effort to publish all @amazon-devices scoped packages to npmjs.

### 5. Prior: LabCorp (6+ years, Tech Lead / Principal Engineer)

- Led AWS cloud migration for LabCorp's [scope]. Designed cloud architecture,
  managed migration roadmap, coordinated across engineering, security, and
  operations teams. Enterprise-scale infrastructure transformation in a
  healthcare/regulated environment.
- End-to-end ML projects in LabCorp Diagnostics (research division):
  problem scoping through deployment in a regulated clinical environment
  with compliance, audit, and data governance requirements.
- Progressed from full-stack developer to Tech Lead to Principal Engineer
  over 6+ years. Healthcare domain experience with controls, approvals,
  auditability, and compliance — directly maps to EPT's enterprise
  requirements.

## How my background maps to the JD

| JD requirement                                     | My evidence                                              |
|----------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| MCP connectors with auth, permissions, retries     | Own a production MCP with dual-audience content firewall |
| Reusable infrastructure other teams build on       | Rescoping framework, now standard for [X] packages       |
| Multi-agent workflows                              | SDK team AI tools (production) + AgentCore (exploratory) |
| Internal apps and integration platforms            | Amazon Devices buildertools — internal dev platform      |
| High ownership in ambiguous environments           | Owned someone else's broken release one month before GA  |
| Enterprise systems: controls, audit, permissions   | Amazon-scale release infra + LabCorp regulated systems   |
| Strong Python                                      | Daily driver alongside TypeScript                        |
| Partner with system owners and business owners     | SAs, evangelists, support, product leadership alignment  |

## What this profile does NOT claim

- I am not a research scientist; I do not publish at NeurIPS.
- The MCP server is not a sophisticated AI system — it is enterprise plumbing
  with an MCP shape. That is the right tool for the job at hand.
- The SDK team AI tools is multi-agent architecture, not autonomous multi-agent.
  The distinction matters and I'm honest about it.
- My AgentCore work is exploratory, not production.

## Stack

Python (daily driver), TypeScript (MCP package, internal tools), Node.js,
npm package authoring, Amazon Brazil/Builder Hub, AWS (SageMaker, Lambda,
ECS, CDK), Docker, Kubernetes, Claude Code, kiro-cli, Amazon Bedrock,
AgentCore, Git, GitHub Actions.
